coredata.dc:title: Complete valorization of bamboo biomass into multifunctional nanomaterials by reactive deep eutectic solvent pretreatment: Towards a waste-free biorefinery
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ying
Sun, Shao-Chao
Zhang, Chen
Ma, Cheng-Ye
Wen, Jia-Long
Yuan, Tong-Qi
coredata.dc:description: The introduction of nanotechnology into conventional biorefinery will facilitate complete valorization of lignocellulosic biomass. In this work, a microwave-assisted deep eutectic solvent (DES) pretreatment was developed for one-pot fractionation of bamboo biomass into three scalable fractions (cellulose-rich residue, lignin and recovered DES), which were then respectively upgraded to three value-added bio-based nanomaterials, namely lignin-containing cellulose nanofibrils (LCNFs), lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) and carbon quantum dots (CDs). After the mild and rapid DES pretreatment (130 °C, 20 min), 51.0% cellulose-rich residue was recovered accompanied by 90.6% delignification ratio. The cellulose-rich residues were mechanically fibrillated into LCNFs with a width of 5–20 nm. In-situ esterification of carboxylic acid groups from DES with cellulose hydroxyl groups improved the hydrophobicity of LCNFs. The LCNFs film prepared exhibited excellent hydrophobic (water contact angle of 103.3°). In-situ esterification, condensation and cleavage reactions of lignin during DES fractionation enhanced its amphiphilicity and facilitated lignin self-assembly into LNPs with diameters of 30–75 nm. Furthermore, using the recovered DES as carbon precursors, CDs were successfully prepared by a typical hydrothermal method, which provides a new approach for the disposal of waste DES. In short, the proposed waste-free biorefinery scheme realizes the complete valorization of bamboo biomass into bio-based multifunctional nanomaterials, which can be further applied in various downstream fields.

head.ce:title: Complete valorization of bamboo biomass into multifunctional nanomaterials by reactive deep eutectic solvent pretreatment: Towards a waste-free biorefinery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The introduction of nanotechnology into conventional biorefinery will facilitate complete valorization of lignocellulosic biomass. In this work, a microwave-assisted deep eutectic solvent (DES) pretreatment was developed for one-pot fractionation of bamboo biomass into three scalable fractions (cellulose-rich residue, lignin and recovered DES), which were then respectively upgraded to three value-added bio-based nanomaterials, namely lignin-containing cellulose nanofibrils (LCNFs), lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) and carbon quantum dots (CDs). After the mild and rapid DES pretreatment (130 °C, 20 min), 51.0% cellulose-rich residue was recovered accompanied by 90.6% delignification ratio. The cellulose-rich residues were mechanically fibrillated into LCNFs with a width of 5–20 nm. In-situ esterification of carboxylic acid groups from DES with cellulose hydroxyl groups improved the hydrophobicity of LCNFs. The LCNFs film prepared exhibited excellent hydrophobic (water contact angle of 103.3°). In-situ esterification, condensation and cleavage reactions of lignin during DES fractionation enhanced its amphiphilicity and facilitated lignin self-assembly into LNPs with diameters of 30–75 nm. Furthermore, using the recovered DES as carbon precursors, CDs were successfully prepared by a typical hydrothermal method, which provides a new approach for the disposal of waste DES. In short, the proposed waste-free biorefinery scheme realizes the complete valorization of bamboo biomass into bio-based multifunctional nanomaterials, which can be further applied in various downstream fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of waste-free biorefinery is highly desiring for improving the overall economies of the current biomass utilization industries. [1,2]. Deconstructing lignocellulosic biomass to obtain scalable building blocks is the key to upgrading biomass into multiple product streams, but this goal is elusive due to the natural heterogeneity and complexity of plant cell walls [3]. Various chemical pretreatment methods have been developed to facilitate the fractionation of biomass components, such as the well-established kraft pulping [4], organic solvent [5], ionic liquid [6,7], acid pretreatment [8,9], and hydrothermally coupled alkali or solvent pretreatment [10]. However, the side reactions of most pretreatments make it difficult to achieve complete utilization of components, such as occurrence of condensation of lignin and degradation of hemicelluloses [11]. In addition, reactive residues containing alkalis, salts, organic solvents are harmful to the environment, and the pretreatment process requires harsh conditions, such as high temperature and high pressure [12]. Therefore, the development of green, mild and low-energy biomass fractionation while realizing the complete valorization of biomass is the goal that researchers in this field have been pursuing.

Deep eutectic solvents (DESs), as a class of green solvents for sustainable fractionation of biomass components, have the advantages of being green, easy synthesis, non-toxicity, and low volatility [13]. During the biomass pretreatment, DESs can efficiently dissociate lignin-carbohydrate complex (LCC) and promote the lignin dissolution [14]. Also, reactive DESs will introduce functional groups such as cations, carboxylic acids, and ammonium during fractionation [15–17]. This simultaneous component fractionation and functionalization will save the consumption of additional chemicals and energy [18]. Different from traditional heating, microwave-assisted heating could promote the rupture of plant cells tissue by causing increased stress on plant cells through non-ionizing radiation [19]. Moreover, it was found that microwave radiation can maximize the ionic characteristics of DES and enhance the polarity of molecules [20]. That is to say, the microwave-assisted DES pretreatment process may help to improve the pretreatment efficiency and promote the occurrence of chemical reactions [21,22]. Additionally, DESs can be recovered and reused in the pretreatment system. However, recovered DES (REDES) inevitably becomes waste after several cycles, mainly because residual sugar and lignin increase the viscosity of DES, resulting in inefficiency of pretreatment. Nevertheless, it is worth noting that these low-content and hard-to-remove brown substances can all be served as carbon precursors, and then upgraded to high value-added carbon materials.

Due to its remarkable structural and environmental advantages, bamboo biomass is considered to be an outstanding contender among lignocellulosic biomass [23,24]. The value-added applications from bamboo cellulose fibers have been widely expanded, such as structural materials [25] and high-strength fibers [26]. Unfortunately, the value-added applications of lignin, hemicelluloses and waste solvents have been neglected. In this work, a waste-free strategy is proposed to upgrade bamboo biomass and waste solvent into bio-based multifunctional nanomaterials. Lactic acid/choline chloride (LA/ChCl) was chosen as the reactive DES to deconstruct bamboo biomass, and a microwave-assisted technique was developed to facilitate the efficiency of fractionation and functionalization. After the fractionation, three precursors for the preparation of nanomaterials were obtained and then upgraded into multifunctional nanomaterials through well-suited methods. (1) Mechanical fibrillation of cellulose-rich residues into esterified lignin-containing cellulose nanofibrils (LCNFs) without additional chemical modification; (2) DES in-situ esterified lignin self-assembled into lignin nanoparticles (LNPs); (3) N-doped carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized from REDES by a typical hydrothermal method. The direct utilization of waste DES will save the additional energy required to repeatedly recover DES from dilute liquid systems. Predictably, this strategy of green, rapid and low energy fractionation of biomass and preparation of multifunctional nanomaterials without additional chemical modification will contribute to the realization of waste-free biorefinery.

Bamboo powder (Neosinocalamus affinis, 40–60 mesh) was provided from a bamboo processing plant in Fujian Province, China. Analytical-grade choline chloride (ChCl, 98%), lactic acid (LA, 85–90%) and other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received.

As in previously reported work [27], DES was synthesized by mixing ChCl and LA at a molar ratio of 1:9 into a flask, followed by heating at 75 °C for 2 h to obtain a homogeneous and transparent liquid. Bamboo powder (4 g) and DES (100 g) were added to a 250 mL two-necked flask, and the flask was transferred to a microwave reaction system (Milestone Srl, Sorisole, Italy) for reaction under constant stirring. The power of microwaves is variable (maximum 300 W), and the system held for 20 min under 100 °C, 110 °C, 120 °C and 130 °C, respectively. The sample was named as “time–temperature”, for example, the sample pretreated for 20 min at 120 °C was named as “20–120”. Sufficient sample was synthesized by following the same experimental procedure, and nanomaterials preparation and systematic characterization were listed detailly in ESI (Supplementary Material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of pretreatment efficiency and characterization of cellulose-rich residues
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a depicts a schematic diagram of the bamboo fractionation and overall upgrading of bamboo biomass into nanomaterials. Three product streams were obtained after the DES fractionation, namely cellulose-rich residue, lignin and REDES. Firstly, as shown in Fig. 1b and 1c, the chemical compositions, solid recovery, delignification and cellulose recovery were measured to evaluate the efficiency of pretreatment. The results showed that the delignification ratio (53.6%-90.6%), hemicelluloses removal (56.4%-91.7%) and cellulose contents (43.9%-81.8%) increased with the elevation of pretreatment temperature, while solid recovery (70.3%-51.0%), residual lignin and hemicelluloses contents in the pretreated substrates were decreased. As a result, the sample (20–130) under the optimal condition (130 °C for 20 min) contained 81.8% cellulose, 3.1% hemicelluloses and 4.5% lignin. Meanwhile, more than 95% of cellulose in bamboo was retained, while around 92.0% of the hemicelluloses was removed. The excellent fractionation efficiency was probably ascribed to the enhanced cleavage of ether bonds in lignin and LCC caused by the direct interaction of electromagnetic radiation with molecules in the reaction mixture [22,28]. Furthermore, the degree of polymerization (DP) of cellulose in the original bamboo biomass and pretreated substrates was comprehensively analyzed (Fig. 1d). The DP value of the pretreated substrates (∼795) was slightly lower than that of original bamboo (∼831), indicating that the cellulose chains were well preserved during the pretreatment process. In short, these results demonstrated that the microwave-assisted DES pretreatment can efficiently fractionate bamboo biomass in a short time without severe degradation of cellulose.

The cellulose-rich residues were analyzed using FTIR and elemental analysis to demonstrate in-situ esterification of the cellulose during the fractionation. As shown in Fig. 1e, by comparing the obtained cellulose substrates (single-line) and their bleached samples (double-line), it was noticed that the carbonyl vibration peak (1730 cm−1) originating from the ester group was both very pronounced. Meanwhile, the estimates from elemental analysis also support the result of esterification (Table S1). It was calculated that the degree of ester group substitution of the bleached samples increased from 0.96 to 1.41. These results indicated that the degree of esterification of cellulose gradually increased with the increase of pretreatment temperature. These ester groups are provided by cellulose hydroxyl groups bonded to carboxylic acid groups of lactic acid during the DES pretreatment [16,29]. The morphology changes of bamboo fibers after the DES pretreatment were observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1f, the original bamboo showed intact bundled fibers and the cell walls remained compact. After the pretreatment, cracks and chips appeared at the fiber ends. As the pretreatment intensity increased, the fiber morphology gradually became looser, and finer fiber bundles appeared instead of fiber fragments, indicating that the cellulose components were not significantly damaged and the non-cellulosic components (lignin and hemicelluloses) were significantly removed. In short, the combination of microwave irradiation and DES achieved efficient deconstruction and fractionation of bamboo biomass. Moreover, the one-step fractionation and chemical modification will effectively save additional energy and chemical consumption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and film properties of LCNFs
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) with moderate hydrophobic modifications are in great demand in related processes [30,31]. The esterification of cellulose always requires an organic solvent as a medium, or repeated drying of the fiber [32,33]. Apparently, serious drawbacks of these techniques are energy-intensive and time-consuming solvent replacement, and desiccation-induced cellulose hornification [34]. As mentioned earlier, microwave-assisted DES pretreatment achieved in-situ esterification of cellulose. Furthermore, the introduction of lignin will endow the material with additional lignin properties, such as hydrophobicity, thermal stability, and UV-blocking properties [35]. Herein, LCNFs with different lignin contents (17.2%, 11.27%, 8.34% and 4.51%) were successfully prepared by simple mechanical fibrillation from cellulose-rich residues. The morphology of LCNFs was observed and the size distribution of LCNFs was counted. As shown in Fig. 2 a-2d, the nanofibers intertwine to form a network. After size statistics (Fig. 2e-2 h), the LCNF samples all exhibited nanoscale widths (12.8 ± 7.6 nm, 11.9 ± 4.0 nm, 9.4 ± 5.3 nm, and 8.8 ± 3.4 nm) and lengths of at least a few microns. Different cellulose-rich residues resulted in LCNFs of different widths under the same homogeneous conditions, suggesting that the width of LCNFs appears to be controlled by the intensity of pretreatment. The XRD patterns of the original bamboo and LCNFs (Fig. 3 a) showed diffraction peaks around 16° (101), 22° (002) and 34° (004), which are typical diffraction peaks of native cellulose I. It was observed that the crystalline structure of cellulose was not changed during the DES fractionation and homogenization process. The CrI value of the original bamboo powder was 39.9%, and that of LCNFs prepared from the cellulose-rich residues (20–100, 20–110, 20–120 and 20–130) continuously increased to 55.2%, 58.9%, 61.8% and 62.9%, respectively. In general, LCNFs with high crystallinity index will contribute to high thermal stability and mechanical properties of LCNF-based materials [36,37].

Tensile strength is the stress at which a material is subjected to maximum uniform plastic deformation. As shown in Fig. 3b and Table S7, the LCNF film from sample 20–130 with the lowest lignin content and the smallest nanofibril size exhibited the best tensile strength (187.6 MPa). The elongation at break reflects the ratio of the length of the material until it breaks under the action of external force to the initial length, indicating the flexibility of LCNF films. The elongation at break values for all films were low (0.7%∼1.0%), which was attributed to the reduction of the number of hydrogen bonds in LCNFs by in-situ esterification of cellulose [38,39]. Meanwhile, the light transmittance of the films in the range of 280 nm to 800 nm was characterized. As shown in Fig. 3c, all the LCNF films were transparent brown due to the presence of residual lignin and exhibited shielding effect to different degrees in the ultraviolet band. The visible light transmittance of the LCNF film at 600 nm was taken as the transparency value of the film and compared. The transparency of LCNF films prepared from 20 to 100, 20–110, 20–120 and 20–130 cellulose-rich residues was 40.6%, 54.5%, 59.2% and 66.6%, respectively. These results indicated that the lignin content and the width of fibers are negatively correlated with the transparency of the LCNF films. Finally, the water contact angle (CA) of the LCNF films was determined to measure the surface wettability of the LCNF films. Fig. 3d is the photographs of water contacting the LCNF films for 5 s. The CA values of LCNF films derived from 20 to 100, 20–110, 20–120 samples gradually decreased from 84.3° to 73.6°. This downward trend was attributed to the removal of lignin during the pretreatment. Interestingly, the CA of 20–130 LCNF film increased significantly to 103.3°, and the hydrophobicity was mainly contributed by the in-situ esterification of cellulose since this sample (20–130) contains the lowest lignin. This CA value is even higher than the composites with CNFs and resins, and the related publications are summarized in Table S2. In summary, the in-situ esterification of cellulose during DES fractionation will effectively save the consumption of additional chemicals and energy for the preparation of hydrophobic LCNFs. Moreover, this degradable, transparent, flexible and hydrophobic LCNF film material is expected to be applied in the field of packaging materials [40].

It is important to elucidate the structure characteristics and functional groups of lignin for its downstream high-value utilization [41]. Herein, the detailed structures of the lignin fractions obtained by the DES fractionation were analyzed by 2D-HSQC (Fig. 4 ) and 31P NMR techniques (Fig. S1), and the native lignin DEL from bamboo (Fig. S2) was also prepared as a control to investigate the lignin structural changes during the DES pretreatment.

In general, the side-chains (δC/δH 50–95/2.5–6.0) of 2D-HSQC spectrum provide information on the substructure (linkages) of the lignin macromolecules, and information on the structural units of the lignin provided by the aromatic regions (δC/δH 95–150/5.6–8.0). The signals in 2D-HSQC spectra were assigned according to previous publications [42–46]. By comparing the 2D-HSQC spectra of the lignin samples (Fig. 4), it was found that all lignin fractions had similar spectral patterns but different signal intensities. In the side-chain region of the spectrum, prominent signals (Aα, Aβ, Aγ) originated from β-aryl-ethers (β-O-4, A) were observed. Meanwhile, a Cγ-Hγ correlations in γ-acylated β-O-4 linkages (A’) was observed at about δC/δH 63.0/4.20, indicating that the γ-carbon of the lignin side-chain in lignin was in-situ acylated with p-coumaric acid in the growth of bamboo [46]. In addition, γ-esterified β-O-4 linkages with lactic acid in the DES (A′) were also appeared in the same area, which was reported in our previous study [44]. Additionally, carbon–carbon linkages, such as β-β and β-5 linkages, were found in side-chain regions such as β-β and β-5. By contrast, the structural units such as syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G), and p-hydroxyphenyl (H), can be distinctly distinguished in the aromatic regions. Strong signals from hydroxycinnamic acids (i.e., p-coumarates, PCE) and ferulate (FA) were also found in all the spectra of lignin, which are typical characteristics of bamboo lignin [47,48]. In addition, Tricin (T) unit previously found in many gramineous also appeared in the spectra of lignin fractions obtained under mild conditions [49–51]. However, T unit was disappeared in the 2D-HSQC spectra due to sufficient cleavage of this acid-sensitive unit from lignin macromolecule during the DES pretreatment.

The structural changes of lignin can be illustrated by quantitative integration of 2D-HSQC NMR characteristic signals. As shown in Table S3, as the pretreatment temperature increased from 100 °C to 130 °C, the content of β-O-4 linkages decreased from 48.7/100Ar to 25.3/100Ar, suggesting that β-O-4 linkages are sensitive to pretreatment temperature. As for the structural units of the lignin, it was noted that S/G ratio of L20-100 was 1.38, while it was increased to 1.43 in L20-110, and 1.61 in L20-130. These results indicated that the G-type lignin units were preferentially degraded or removed during the acidic DES pretreatment, which are in agreement with some acidic DES pretreatments [44,52,53]. In addition, the proportion of H unit increased from 11.3/100Ar in L20-100 to 24.5/100Ar in L130-20, indicating that of lignin was prone to demethylation at relative higher temperatures. This increased H unit is beneficial to increase the active site of lignin and synthesis of lignin-based materials [54,55]. Likewise, the content of tricin unit (T) also decreased from 7.28/100Ar in L100-20 to 0.65/100Ar in L120-20. This is because the T units are bonded to the α-OH in the lignin side-chain through ether bonds, which are unstable in acidic DES [50,56].

In addition to structural features, the chemical reactivity of lignin fractions (OH and COOH groups) is also an important indicator to verify lignin chemical modification and value-added applications. As shown in Table 1 , the content of aliphatic OH groups in the DES lignin fractions (L20-100 to L20-130) gradually decreased with the elevation of pretreatment temperature, which implied that aliphatic OH groups could interact with lactic acid in DES system. In fact, the decreased aliphatic OH suggested that esterification of lignin can take place at aliphatic OH groups during the DES fractionation. Simultaneously, the lactic acid signal (L2) detected in 2D-HSQC spectra of lignin also implied that the γ-OH group in the lignin substructure had undergone in-situ esterification reaction with the COOH group in LA. By contrast, the total content of phenolic OH groups (especially S-type) in the DES lignin fraction increased remarkably from 1.88 mmol/g lignin to 2.84 mmol/g lignin, suggesting that cleavage of β-O-4 linkages during the DES pretreatment resulted in the generation of new phenolic OH groups [57,58]. These significantly increased phenolic OH groups and in-situ esterification in these lignin fractions implied that their amphiphilicity are significantly enhanced under the high temperature DES fractionation.

The utilization of lignin is considered as an important process for sustainable biorefinery [59,60]. Large particle size, inhomogeneity and poor dispersion are the main limiting factors for the application of lignin as a high value-added material [61]. The introduction of nanotechnology in the field of lignin applications has broken the technological boundaries. A typical LNP formation process is shown in Fig. 5 a. Lignin forms a homogeneous solution and molecular associations in the solvent, and the subsequent introduction of water as an anti-solvent reduces the solubility of lignin. In order to reduce the contact area with the anti-solvent, the hydrophobic structure of lignin aggregates inward through self-assembly, and the hydrophilic functional groups extend outward to form spherical particles [62,63]. Therefore, the amphiphilic structure of lignin plays an important role in the formation of LNPs [64]. As shown in Fig. 5b, LNPs prepared from different temperature-extracted lignin were brown, and the color darken as the pretreatment temperature increased. The morphology and size distribution of LNPs prepared from DES lignin were characterized by TEM technique. As shown in Fig. 5c-5f, the LNPs are all nano-scale spherical (<100 nm) with good dispersion. After the size statistics of LNPs, as shown in Fig. 5g-5j, the average size of LNPs decreased from 63.6 nm to 36.4 nm with the increase of pretreatment temperature. The results showed that LNPs with regular size-shape were successfully prepared from DES lignin. In addition, it was found that the lignin extracted at high temperature seems to favor the formation of smaller LNPs, and the structure of lignin seems to directly affects the size of LNPs.

Earlier work indicated that additional hydrophobic modification (acetylation or esterification) of lignin can enhance its amphiphilicity, which is more conducive to the preparation of LNPs with regular size and shape [61,65]. In this work, the DES fractionation achieved in-situ esterification of lignin. By combining the NMR structure information of lignin, the relationship between lignin structure and LNP size was further elucidated. As shown in Table S3, as the pretreatment temperature increased, the relative contents of H units in the lignin gradually increased (S/G/H changed from 51/37/11 to 47/29/25). Previous studies have shown that the strong π-π interaction between lignin macromolecules is highly correlated with the abundance of lignin aromatic rings [66], and this interaction force increases in the order of S, G, and H units [67,68]. LNPs from high-temperature-extracted DES lignin exhibited smaller sizes, which was attributed to the increased S/G value and the proportion of H units (Table S3, S/G/H), resulting in stronger π-π interactions promoting tighter packing of lignin molecules. At the same time, according to the results of NMR analysis, the amphiphilicity was enhanced through the following reactions during the DES pretreatment, which promoted the self-assembly process of LNPs to form smaller particles: 1) In-situ esterification of γ-OH groups of lignin with COOH in LA (hydrophobic group); 2) condensation of lignin at high temperature, and 3) cleavage reaction of lignin to expose more phenolic OH of lignin (hydrophilic group) [69]. Herein, the surface charges of LNPs from L20-130 were also determined by zeta (ζ) potential analysis to evaluate the stability of LNPs in different pH environments. As shown in Fig. S3, when pH＜3, the charged functional groups of LNPs were protonated, resulting in a significant decrease in the repulsion of the electric double layer, thus the LNPs were in an unstable state [65]. The ζ-potential values at pH 5 ∼ 13 confirmed the high stability of the LNP dispersions in this range (-5.54 mV ∼ 20.02 mV). LNP was most stable at pH 12 with a the maximum ζ-potential of −20.02 mV. In summary, the DES pretreatment process led to chemical reactions such as chemical modification, cleavage reaction, and condensation of lignin. The relative increase in the number of hydrophobic and hydrophilic functional groups in lignin indicated the enhanced amphipathicity of fractionated lignin fragments, which facilitated the self-assembly of lignin into LNPs. Such smaller and stable LNPs are expected to be used in applications requiring small-sized LNPs, such as drug delivery, water treatment, and nanofillers [70–72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs from recycled DES
ce:section.ce:para: DES is a colorless and transparent liquid, and the recovered DES (REDES) is brown after pretreatment (inset of Fig. 6 a). The 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S4) of DES and REDES had no obvious difference, indicating that the residual sugar and lignin content were less, below the detection limit of NMR. Therefore, using UV spectrophotometer and HPLC, it was determined that the main substances in REDES were lactic acid, xylose, glucose, lignin and HMF, and the specific contents are summarized in Table S5. Firstly, N-doped CDs were prepared from REDES through a typical hydrothermal process. The original DES was used as a comparison to demonstrate the key roles of residual sugars and lignin in REDES. As shown in the Fig. 6a, REDES-CDs shows a broad absorption from 200 to 550 nm according to the UV–vis spectrum. The absorption peak at 280 nm indicated the existence of a π-π* transitions, possibly related to the C = C bond [73]. A weak absorption peak at ∼320 nm indicated the presence of an n-π ∗ transitions [74], which could be attributed to the C = O structure in CDs. However, these absorption peaks were almost absent in DES-CDs. As shown in the inset in Fig. 6b, the DES-CDs showed faint blue light under the irradiation of UV light (365 nm), while the CDs with REDES as the precursor showed a distinct blue light. The optical properties of the CDs were confirmed by fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 6b and 6c, the optimal excitation wavelength (EX) of REDES-CDs was 322 nm, and the corresponding emission wavelength (EM) was 430 nm, which is located in the blue region of the visible spectrum. The EX and EM of DES-CDs was 330 nm and 435 nm, respectively. However, it was clear that there was a significant difference in their fluorescence intensities, with DES-CDs (12.03) being lower than REDES-CDs (62.93). In addition, the difference in the quantum yields between DES-CDs (1.55%) and REDES-CDs (4.93%) further demonstrated the superiority of REDES as a starting material for CDs preparation. In summary, these waste sugars and lignin, which led to the troublesome recyclability of DES, can be successfully upgraded to CDs via a hydrothermal process as carbon precursors with surface hydroxyl or carboxyl groups.

The morphology and structure of CDs from REDES were further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 7 a and 7c, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and AFM image illustrated that the CDs have a nearly spherical morphology and a uniform structure with an average particle size of 9.81 nm (Fig. 7b). The hexagons in the fast Fourier transform image (FFT) represented the complete crystal planes of the CDs with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. S5), which is consistent with the (100) crystal plane of graphene [75]. From the Raman spectrum of the REDES-CDs (Fig. 7d), it can be seen that the two characteristic peaks centered at ∼1360 cm−1 and ∼1590 cm−1 belong to the D and G bands of carbon materials, indicating the existence of nanocrystalline graphitic sp2 and sp3 carbon defects in CDs [76,77]. The ID/IG ratio (the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbons) was 1.276, indicating that the extent of N-induced disorder and the defects in graphitic arrangement in N-doped CDs [78].

The chemical composition and surface functional groups of CDs were determined using XPS and FTIR. The wide-scan XPS spectra of REDES-CDs (Fig. S6) showed that the presence of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in the REDES-CDs can be obviously located at around 285 eV, 532 eV and 400 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7e, the XPS spectrum of C1s depicted three main peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.04 eV, corresponding to C = C (57.37%), C-O/C-N (22.07%) and O-C = O (20.56%), respectively [79]. As shown in Fig. 7f, the O1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks, and the peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.4 eV and 533.96 eV were assigned to C = O, C-OH/C-O-C and COOH, respectively [80]. Three main peaks appeared in the N1s band (Fig. 7g), which were assigned to N-H (398.0 eV, pyridine nitrogen), C-N/C = N (399. 77 eV, graphitic nitrogen) and ammonium (407.1 eV) [81]. XPS results showed that REDES-CD have a carbon–carbon skeleton, and the surface is rich in bonding of oxygen-containing groups (such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, etc.) and nitrogen elements (provided by choline chloride), indicating that N-doped CDs were successfully prepared by the REDES. The FTIR spectra of the REDES-CDs were also analyzed. As depicted in Fig. 7h, FTIR spectrum indicated the existence of bonds of hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and amino groups on REDES-CDs’ surface. The broad peaks around 3400 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H [82]. At 1580–1720 cm−1, the peaks attributed to C = N and C = C appeared [83]. The peaks centered at 1384 cm−1, 1250 cm-1and 1050 cm−1 were closely related to the C-OH, C-N and C-O-C stretching vibrations, respectively [84].

Based on the structural information obtained from the above characterizations, the formation of N-doped CDs may have undergone sequential processes, as shown in Fig. 7i, including hydrolysis, dehydration, polymerization, doping, aromatization, carbonization and nucleation. REDES contained amorphous carbohydrates and lignin fragments extracted from bamboo. Under high temperature and pressure, these carbohydrates were first hydrolyzed into oligomers, and the lignin fragments were degraded into various small molecules such as aromatic acids, phenols and aromatic ethers. Then, these compounds undergone dehydration, condensation, chain scission, and chain formation reactions with lactic acid, choline chloride, etc., to converted into a series of heterocyclic compounds with polycyclic rings, which were then aromatized and carbonized through condensation and cycloaddition reactions [69]. Finally, by further prolonging the reaction time, aromatic clusters formed CDs by burst nucleation when the mixture concentration reached the critical supersaturation point [85]. Overall, the above results demonstrated for the first time the feasibility of producing CDs from biomass-pretreated REDES. This green deconstruction process and waste-free nanomaterials upscaling will provide an effective strategy for complete biorefinery.

In this study, a microwave-assisted DES pretreatment rapidly fractionated bamboo biomass and achieved in-situ chemical modification of cellulose and lignin. The optimized pretreatment condition (130 °C, 20 min) achieved over 90% delignification and retained almost all cellulose. LCNFs exhibits a high aspect ratio, and in-situ esterification of cellulose renders the LCNF film hydrophobic (contact angle 103.3°). Simultaneously, in-situ esterification during the DES pretreatment enhanced the amphiphilicity of lignin and facilitated their self-assembly into smaller-sized LNPs with an average particle size of 36.4 nm. Furthermore, the waste DES can be effectively used as carbon precursors for the preparation of CDs, which provided a new utilization route for the waste DES. This sustainable process achieved full utilization of bamboo and upgraded biomass and pretreatment solvent to nanomaterials, which provided a scalable platform for the valuation of a waste-free biorefinery.
